focus_areas: File serving & path traversal (routes/fileServer.ts, logfileServer.ts, quarantineServer.ts)

findings.title: SQL injection in login enables authentication bypass
findings.description: login.ts:31 builds a raw query by string-interpolating req.body.email directly: `SELECT * FROM Users WHERE email = '${req.body.email}' AND password = '${security.hash(...)}' ...` and runs it via models.sequelize.query. The email is fully attacker-controlled and unescaped, so a payload like `' OR 1=1--` bypasses authentication and logs in as the first user (typically admin).
findings.exploit_scenario: POST /rest/user/login with {"email":"' OR 1=1--","password":"x"} authenticates as admin.
findings.recommendation: Use parameterized queries / Sequelize model methods; never interpolate request data into SQL.
findings.confidence_reason: Unescaped request data interpolated into raw SQL on an unauthenticated endpoint; textbook injection.

findings.title: SQL injection in product search enables full DB exfiltration
findings.description: search.ts:19 interpolates the search criteria into a raw query: `SELECT * FROM Products WHERE ((name LIKE '%${criteria}%' OR description LIKE '%${criteria}%') ...)`. criteria derives from the `q` request parameter, unescaped, allowing UNION-based extraction of arbitrary tables (users, cards). search.ts:43 additionally exposes `SELECT sql FROM sqlite_master` to aid schema discovery.
findings.exploit_scenario: GET /rest/products/search?q=')) UNION SELECT ... FROM Users-- dumps user credentials.
findings.recommendation: Parameterize the query or use the ORM; bind the criteria as a value.
findings.confidence_reason: Unauthenticated raw-SQL interpolation of a request parameter.

findings.title: Hardcoded RSA private key allows JWT forgery for any role
findings.description: insecurity.ts:17 embeds the RSA private key as a string literal in source. authorize() (line 50) signs JWTs with it (RS256). Because the signing key is public knowledge, an attacker can mint a valid token for any user/role (e.g. role=admin), defeating the entire auth scheme.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker signs {data:{role:'admin'}} with the committed key and presents it; server-side verify() accepts it.
findings.recommendation: Remove the key from source; load from a secret store; rotate the key.
findings.confidence_reason: Private signing key committed in plaintext; directly forgeable tokens.

findings.title: Path traversal in file download serves arbitrary server files
findings.description: fileServer.ts:30 does res.sendFile(path.resolve('ftp/', file)) where `file` comes from the URL. path.resolve with a traversal payload (or absolute path) escapes the ftp/ directory; juice-shop's classic poison-null-byte/`..%2f` variants reach files outside ftp/. logfileServer.ts:11 and quarantineServer.ts:11 follow the same pattern.
findings.exploit_scenario: GET /ftp/..%2f..%2fpackage.json (or null-byte variants) reads files outside the intended directory.
findings.recommendation: Reject '..'/absolute paths; verify the resolved path stays within the base dir before sendFile.
findings.confidence_reason: User-controlled path into sendFile with only a base-dir prefix; reachable traversal.

findings.title: XXE local file disclosure via uploaded XML parsed with noent:true
findings.description: fileUpload.ts:80 parses uploaded XML with libxml.parseXml(data, { noent: true, ... }) inside a vm. noent:true enables external general entity substitution, so a SYSTEM entity (e.g. file:///etc/passwd) is expanded and reflected back in the error/response (the code explicitly checks the result against /etc/passwd at line 82).
findings.exploit_scenario: Upload an XML with <!DOCTYPE foo [<!ENTITY x SYSTEM 'file:///etc/passwd'>]><foo>&x;</foo>; the file contents are disclosed in the response.
findings.recommendation: Disable entity substitution (noent:false) and DTD loading for untrusted XML.
findings.confidence_reason: Entity substitution explicitly enabled on attacker-uploaded XML; the disclosure path is reflected back.

findings.title: Passwords hashed with unsalted MD5
findings.description: insecurity.ts:37: hash() = crypto.createHash('md5').update(data).digest('hex'). MD5 is fast and broken for password storage, and there is no per-user salt, enabling rainbow-table and brute-force recovery of credentials once the DB is exfiltrated (e.g. via F-001/F-002).
findings.exploit_scenario: After dumping the Users table via SQLi, MD5 hashes are cracked offline in seconds.
findings.confidence_reason: Unsalted MD5 for credentials is unambiguous; impact gated on obtaining the hashes.

findings.title: Server-side request forgery via profile-image URL fetch
findings.description: profileImageUrlUpload.ts:21 performs await fetch(url) on req.body.imageUrl with no scheme/host validation. An authenticated user can make the server issue requests to arbitrary internal or cloud-metadata endpoints (e.g. http://169.254.169.254/...), exfiltrating responses or probing the intranet.
findings.exploit_scenario: POST profile image URL = http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ to reach cloud metadata.
findings.recommendation: Allowlist schemes (https) and resolved host ranges; block link-local/private IPs before fetch.
findings.confidence_reason: User-controlled URL passed straight to server-side fetch; standard SSRF, requires auth.

findings.title: Hardcoded HMAC secret weakens token/coupon integrity
findings.description: insecurity.ts:38 uses a hardcoded HMAC key 'pa4qacea4VK9t9nGv7yZtwmj' for hmac(). A static, committed secret lets an attacker forge any HMAC the application trusts.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker computes valid HMACs for values the server believes only it can sign.
findings.recommendation: Move the secret to per-deployment configuration and rotate it.
findings.confidence_reason: Committed static HMAC key; impact depends on what the HMAC gates.

findings.title: Open redirect via substring-based allowlist
findings.description: isRedirectAllowed (insecurity.ts:128-133) checks `url.includes(allowedUrl)` for each allowlist entry. A substring match passes any URL that merely contains an allowlisted string, e.g. http://evil.example/?x=https://github.com/juice-shop/juice-shop, which redirect.ts then passes to res.redirect.
findings.exploit_scenario: GET /redirect?to=http://evil.example/?x=https://github.com/juice-shop/juice-shop redirects the victim to attacker-controlled host.
findings.recommendation: Match the redirect target by exact origin, not substring.
findings.confidence_reason: includes()-based allowlist is trivially bypassable; classic open redirect (lower impact class).

findings.title: JWT decoded without verification and consumed in role checks
findings.description: decode() (insecurity.ts:52) returns jws.decode(token).payload with NO signature check. Role checks (insecurity.ts:150-166) use `verify(...) && decode(...)`, and verify() relies on jws.verify with the public key; combined with the hardcoded private key (F-003), and given decode-derived data feeds authorization, the token trust chain is broken (forgery / algorithm-confusion).
findings.exploit_scenario: Forged or alg-confused token yields a decoded payload whose role grants privileged access.
findings.recommendation: Verify with a pinned algorithm before reading any claim; never authorize on unverified decode output.
findings.confidence_reason: decode-without-verify is real; exact exploitability depends on which gate is authoritative at runtime.

findings.title: Denial of service via YAML bomb on file upload
findings.description: fileUpload.ts:114 runs yaml.load(data) on an uploaded YAML inside a vm with a 2s timeout. A YAML bomb (recursive anchor expansion) exhausts CPU/memory; the code itself recognizes the 'yamlBomb'/'Invalid string length' failure mode, confirming the path is reachable.
findings.exploit_scenario: Upload a recursively-anchored YAML ('billion laughs') to spike CPU/memory.
findings.recommendation: Cap input size and expansion depth before parsing; use a safe loader with limits.
findings.confidence_reason: Algorithmic-expansion DoS (reportable class), mitigated only by a 2s timeout.

findings.title: Server-side expression evaluation / DoS via safeEval of B2B order data
findings.description: b2bOrder.ts:20 runs vm.runInContext('safeEval(orderLinesData)', sandbox, {timeout:2000}) where orderLinesData is attacker-supplied. It relies on `notevil`'s safeEval plus a vm timeout; historically such sandboxes are escapable, and at minimum attacker expressions can cause CPU-exhaustion DoS.
findings.exploit_scenario: Submit crafted orderLinesData to attempt sandbox escape (RCE) or guaranteed CPU exhaustion.
findings.recommendation: Do not eval untrusted input; parse order data with a strict schema/allowlist.
findings.confidence_reason: eval of untrusted data is a strong smell; RCE depends on a notevil/vm escape, so confidence is moderate (DoS is near-certain).

findings.title: Path traversal in log/quarantine file serving
findings.description: logfileServer.ts:11 (res.sendFile(path.resolve('logs/', file))) and quarantineServer.ts:11 (ftp/quarantine/) repeat the fileServer.ts traversal pattern with a user-controlled path and only a base-dir prefix.
findings.exploit_scenario: Traversal payload on the logs/quarantine route reaches files outside the intended directory.
findings.recommendation: Same as F-004: confine the resolved path to the base directory.
findings.confidence_reason: Same mechanism as F-004; separate routes/assets so reported distinctly.

findings.title: Database schema disclosure via sqlite_master query
findings.description: search.ts:43 executes `SELECT sql FROM sqlite_master` and surfaces the DB schema, easing exploitation of the SQL injection in F-002. On its own it is information disclosure (table/column names).
findings.exploit_scenario: Schema names returned guide UNION-based extraction in F-002.
findings.recommendation: Remove the schema-dumping branch; do not expose sqlite_master.
findings.confidence_reason: Real disclosure but low standalone impact; primarily an aid to F-002.
